memoryArtifacts.description:
BIO:  from Burkes's Peerage & Baronetage, 1999, p. 161

Sir Richard Bagot, of Bagot's Bromley; living 1256; m. Christian, daughter of Sir William Mauveslin, of Rydeware, and d. by June 1271.

zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromley於1231年出生在英國英格蘭斯塔福爾巴格也斯 布羅姆利時，他的父親Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromley35歲，母親Alicia Bigod22歲。他於大約1254年與Christiana Mauveslin在英國英格蘭結婚。他們至少是2個兒子和5個女兒的父母。他去世於1271年6月之前在英國英格蘭，埋葬在英國英格蘭格盧斯特斯爾薩培頓。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromley于1231年出生在英国英格兰斯塔福爾巴格也斯 布羅姆利时，他的父亲Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromley35岁，母亲Alicia Bigod22岁。他于大约1254年与Christiana Mauveslin在英国英格兰结婚。他们至少是2个儿子和5个女儿的父母。他去世于1271年6月之前在英国英格兰，埋葬在英国英格兰格盧斯特斯爾薩培頓。

nameMeanings.name: Bagot,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Lancashire): variant of   Baggett   (see also   Baggott  ), from a pet form of   Bagg   2.

variants.name: Baggett
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Norman personal name Bagot, a diminutive of ancient Germanic Bago, from the root bag- ‘to fight’. Compare Bagg 2.

nameMeanings.name: Lord
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: nickname from Middle English laford, louerd, lord ‘lord, master’ (Old English hlāford), perhaps given to a someone who played the part of the lord or master of ceremonies in festival games.
Irish: adoption of the English name (see 1 above) as a translation of the main element of Gaelic Ó Tighearnaigh (see Tierney ) and Mac Thighearnáin (see McKiernan ).
Americanized form of French Laure, a variant of the personal name Laur 3.

variants.name: Tierney
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Eamon, John Patrick, Brigid, Clodagh, Kieran, Michael Sean, Siobhan.

Irish: shortened form of O'Tierney, an Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó Tíghearnaigh ‘descendant of Tighearnach’, a byname meaning ‘lord, master’. The Gaelic name is sometimes ‘translated’ as English Lord . Compare Tiernan .

variants.name: Lords
meanings: English: variant of Lord , with post-medieval excrescent -s. This surname is rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Kyong, Kyong Suk, Young, Chae, Chi Young, Hang, Kil, Rin, Sang Hun, Chang, Chong, Insoo, Kum, Sun Young, Yom, Young Hwan.  Cambodian Kiwan, Oeurn, Phoeun, Pich, Saroeuth, Seung, Sophath, Sophea, Yoeun, Phan, Son.  Indian Shanti, Harendra.
Indian (Uttar Pradesh): Brahmin name, from Sanskrit om, the Hindu sacred and mystic syllable uttered at the beginning of prayers.
Cambodian: written ឱម, corresponding to the exclamation of solemn affirmation, of Sanskrit origin (see 1 above), chanted by Buddhist monks.

variants.name: Oum
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Heang, Hye, Kam, Kan, Kheng, Khin, Kun, Sang, Sok, Phong, Yom, Yoo, Youn, Cuong, Phal, Samnang, Tin.  Cambodian Sokha, Chheang, Choeun, Moeun, Roeun, Sambath, Samoeun, Savuth, Soeun, Sopheap.
Cambodian: written អ៊ុំ, from a Khmer word denoting an elder sibling of one's parent (i.e. ‘uncle’ or ‘aunt’). Compare   Om   3 and   Um   2.

nameMeanings.name: Bromley
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from any of the many places called Bromley, in Essex, Hertfordshire, Kent (now in Greater London), Greater London, Greater Manchester, Staffordshire, Shropshire, and elsewhere. Most are named with Old English brōm ‘broom’ + lēah ‘woodland clearing’, but Bromley (near Bow) in Greater London is from Old English bræmbel ‘bramble’ + lēah.

variants.name: Brimley
meanings: habitational name, perhaps from Brimley in Devon or Brimbley in Stoke Abbott, Dorset, both named with Old English brōm ‘broom’ + lēah ‘woodland clearing’.
variant of   Bromley  .
English:

variants.name: Brumley
meanings: English (Lancashire): variant of   Bromley  . This surname is rare in Britain.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: 100 Years of Conflict Between England and France Ends
historicEvents.description: Treaty of Paris was signed on December 4, 1259, which ended 100 years of conflict between England and France.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromley was born in 1231, in Bagot's Bromley, Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom, his father, Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromley, was 35 and his mother, Alicia Bigod, was 22. He married Christiana Mauveslin about 1254, in England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 2 sons and 5 daughters. He died before June 1271, in England, United Kingdom, and was buried in Sapperton, Gloucestershire, England, United Kingdom.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromley est né en 1231, à Bagot's Bromley, Staffordshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, son père, Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromley, avait 35 ans et sa mère, Alicia Bigod, avait 22 ans. Il a épousé Christiana Mauveslin environ 1254, à Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils et 5 filles. Il est décédé avant juin 1271, à Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, et a été enterré à Sapperton, Gloucestershire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromley 1231 in Bagot's Bromley, Staffordshire, England, United Kingdom geboren wurde, war sein Vater Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromley 35 und seine Mutter Alicia Bigod 22. Er hat um 1254 in England, United Kingdom Christiana Mauveslin geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 2 Söhne und 5 Töchter. Er starb vor Juni 1271 in England, United Kingdom und wurde in Sapperton, Gloucestershire, England, United Kingdom begraben.

Lui sposato Christiana Mauveslin circa 1254, nel Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 2 figli e 5 figlie. ja.lifeSummary: Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromleyが1231年にイギリスイギリススタフォードシュアイアバゴト'ス ブロムリーで生まれた時，父Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromleyは35歳，母Alicia Bigodは22歳であった。約1254年にイギリスイギリスでChristiana Mauveslinと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも2人の息子と5人の娘の親であった。彼は1271年6月以前にイギリスイギリスで死去し，イギリスイギリスグロウセステルシュアイアサップエルトンにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromley(이)가 1231년 영국 잉글랜드 스태퍼드실르 바고트'스 브롬리에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromley(은)는 35세였고, 그의 어머니 Alicia Bigod(은)는 22세였다. 그는 대략 1254년 영국 잉글랜드에서 Christiana Mauveslin(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들과 다섯 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1271년 6월 이전 영국 잉글랜드에서 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 그루스스터실르 사퍼턴에 묻혔다.

Ele casou-se com Christiana Mauveslin aproximadamente 1254, em Inglaterra, United Kingdom. Eles tiveram pelo menos 2 filhos e 5 filhas. ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sir Richard Bagot, Lord of Bromley родился в 1231 г. в местности багот'с бромли, стаффордшир, Англия, Великобритания, его отцу по имени Sir Hugh Bagot, Lord of Bromley было 35 лет, а матери, которую звали Alicia Bigod, 22. Он женился на женщине по имени Christiana Mauveslin около 1254 г. в местности Англия, Великобритания. У них было по крайней мере 2 сына и 5 дочерей. Он умер перед июнь 1271 г. в местности Англия, Великобритания и был похоронен в местности саппиртон, глоусестиршир, Англия, Великобритания.

Murió antes de junio de 1271, en Inglaterra, Reino Unido, y fue sepultado en Sapperton, Gloucestershire, Inglaterra, Reino Unido.
